title: Pair-Partition Constructions for CPM-Based Quantum LDPC Codes
abstract: We construct binary Calderbank--Shor--Steane (CSS) quantum low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes from circulant permutation matrices (CPMs). The construction is parameterized by column weight J, row weight L, and prime lift size P. A J x J array of pair partitions imposes linear paired-difference equations on the CPM exponents. These equations give CSS orthogonality. The main finite examples reported here are the (J,L)=(4,12)-regular girth-six code [[372,130,16]] with rate 0.349, and the (J,L)=(4,14)-regular girth-six code [[518,228,16]] with rate 0.440. We also record (J,L)=(3,8)-regular girth-six instances [[472,122,14]] and [[488,126,14]], with lift sizes P=59 and P=61, respectively. The stated distances are established for the fixed matrices by exhaustive low-weight exclusion together with explicit non-stabilizer witnesses.

bite.hook: New quantum LDPC codes achieve a distance of 16 with rates up to 0.440 using circulant permutation matrices.
tldr: They constructed binary CSS quantum LDPC codes using circulant permutation matrices parameterized by column/row weights and prime lift sizes.
They applied a J x J array of pair partitions to impose linear paired-difference equations on CPM exponents to ensure CSS orthogonality.
They discovered regular girth-six codes including a [[372,130,16]] code and a [[518,228,16]] code, verifying distances via low-weight exclusion.
bite.whyItMatters: Quantum low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes are crucial for building fault-tolerant quantum computers because they dramatically reduce the physical qubit overhead needed for error correction.
bite.eli5: Imagine trying to send a secret message where some letters might get smudged. By arranging the letters in a highly structured grid using mathematical rules, we can easily spot and fix any errors. This research finds a new, highly efficient grid pattern to protect fragile quantum information from getting corrupted.

glossary.shortDef: A class of quantum error-correcting codes constructed from two classical linear codes.
glossary.eli5Def: A special recipe for quantum security that fixes bit flips and phase flips separately using classical computer rules.

glossary.shortDef: Low-density parity-check codes where each parity check involves only a small number of code symbols.
glossary.eli5Def: An error-checking system where each clue only looks at a few letters, making it super fast and easy to solve.

glossary.shortDef: A square matrix where each row is a cyclic shift of the row above it by one element.
glossary.eli5Def: A grid of numbers where each row is just the previous row shifted over by one spot, like a repeating loop.
